mechanism_narrative: A rising trend in industrial production over the prior three months indicates expanding manufacturing output and capacity utilization, which translates into higher corporate revenue and earnings, driving positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: Future 20-day S&P 500 returns are not positively correlated with 3-month INDPRO momentum (Spearman rank correlation < 0.05) over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A labor market threshold where the unemployment rate falls below 5.0 percent signals an overheating labor market that increases wage pressure and tightening risk, leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days as discount rates reprice higher.
failure_condition: In periods when UNRATE is below 5.0%, the subsequent 60-day S&P 500 return is positive in more than 55% of observations, or the mean return is not significantly negative (t-test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Concurrent month-over-month increases in retail sales and housing starts signal synchronized consumer and investment demand expansion that lifts aggregate corporate profits, producing positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days only when both series rise together.
failure_condition: The joint condition of rising RSAFS and rising HOUST fails to generate a higher probability of positive 20-day returns than either series alone (interaction term coefficient insignificant at 10% level in logistic regression).

mechanism_narrative: During monetary tightening cycles, stronger-than-trend payroll growth (PAYEMS) raises the expected path of the federal funds rate and compresses equity valuations, generating negative S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days.
failure_condition: During identified tightening cycles, months with above-median PAYEMS growth show average 5-day S&P 500 returns that are not significantly negative (t-test p > 0.10) or outperform below-median months.

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP (GDPC1) exceeding a 2.5 percent annualized growth threshold indicates robust aggregate demand that supports corporate revenue expansion and drives positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days.
failure_condition: Quarters with annualized GDPC1 growth above 2.5% are followed by negative 60-day S&P 500 returns in more than 50% of instances, or the mean return is not positive (t-test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneous month-over-month increases in the federal funds rate and the consumer price index amplify the real interest rate shock, tightening financial conditions and producing negative S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days beyond the independent effects of either series.
failure_condition: The interaction term of FEDFUNDS and CPIAUCSL changes does not significantly predict negative 5-day returns (p > 0.10 in a panel regression with controls), or the joint signal underperforms single-series signals.

mechanism_narrative: An upward trend in the consumer price index signals building inflationary pressure that raises the risk premium on future cash flows and leads to negative S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days as markets anticipate monetary policy repricing.
failure_condition: Months with rising CPIAUCSL show no significant negative drift in subsequent 20-day S&P 500 returns (mean return not negative at 10% significance), or rank correlation between CPI momentum and returns is positive.

mechanism_narrative: In the early post-recession recovery phase, month-over-month payroll employment gains (PAYEMS) confirm escaping hysteresis and drive positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days as earnings expectations reflate.
failure_condition: During post-recession recovery periods defined by unemployment above 6% and recent negative GDP growth, above-median PAYEMS changes do not produce positive 60-day returns (t-test p > 0.10) or are followed by negative returns in a majority of instances.
